[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Heian-ky%C5%8D%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Heian-ky%C5%8D%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Heian-ky%C5%8D%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Heian-ky%C5%8D%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(平安京; lit. 'peaceful/tranquil capital') was one of several former names for the city now known as [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"). It was the *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* [capital of Japan](/wiki/Capital_of_Japan "Capital of Japan") for over one thousand years, from 794 to 1869.[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, Heian-kyō was never explicitly abandoned as the capital. Today, it is said that Tokyo has effectively replaced Kyoto as the capital, but there is a viewpoint that, in theory, Kyoto still holds the position of the capital.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Emperor Saga](/wiki/Emperor_Saga "Emperor Saga") also declared that Heian-kyō would remain the capital forever.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Capital of Japan from 794 to 1868

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Heiankyo_overall_model.jpg/250px-Heiankyo_overall_model.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heiankyo_overall_model.jpg)

Scale model of Heian-kyō

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Daidairi_of_Heiankyo.jpg/250px-Daidairi_of_Heiankyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daidairi_of_Heiankyo.jpg)

Daidairi (大内裏, palace in the center) and the cityscape of Heian-kyō (miniature model at the Kyoto City Life-long Learning Center)

**Heian-kyō** (平安京; lit. 'peaceful/tranquil capital') was one of several former names for the city now known as [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"). It was the *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* [capital of Japan](/wiki/Capital_of_Japan "Capital of Japan") for over one thousand years, from 794 to 1869.[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, Heian-kyō was never explicitly abandoned as the capital. Today, it is said that Tokyo has effectively replaced Kyoto as the capital, but there is a viewpoint that, in theory, Kyoto still holds the position of the capital.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Emperor Saga](/wiki/Emperor_Saga "Emperor Saga") also declared that Heian-kyō would remain the capital forever.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Emperor Kanmu](/wiki/Emperor_Kanmu "Emperor Kanmu") established it as the capital in 794, moving the [Imperial Court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto") there from nearby [Nagaoka-kyō](/wiki/Nagaoka-ky%C5%8D "Nagaoka-kyō") at the recommendation of his advisor [Wake no Kiyomaro](/wiki/Wake_no_Kiyomaro "Wake no Kiyomaro") and marking the beginning of the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") of Japanese history.[[4]](#cite_note-Hall-4) According to modern scholarship, the city is thought to have been modelled after the [urban planning](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_urban_planning "Ancient Chinese urban planning") for the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") Chinese capital of [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") (modern-day [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ebrey_103-6) It remained the chief political center until 1185, when the samurai [Minamoto](/wiki/Minamoto "Minamoto") clan defeated the [Taira](/wiki/Taira "Taira") clan in the [Genpei War](/wiki/Genpei_War "Genpei War"), moving administration of national affairs to [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura "Kamakura") and establishing the [Kamakura shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate").

Though political power would be wielded by the samurai class over the course of three different [shogunates](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun"), Heian-kyō remained the site of the Imperial Court and seat of Imperial power, and was thus the official capital.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heian-ky%C5%8D&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/%E8%A5%BF%E5%AF%BA%E5%BE%A9%E5%85%83%E6%A8%A1%E5%9E%8B.jpg/250px-%E8%A5%BF%E5%AF%BA%E5%BE%A9%E5%85%83%E6%A8%A1%E5%9E%8B.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%A5%BF%E5%AF%BA%E5%BE%A9%E5%85%83%E6%A8%A1%E5%9E%8B.jpg)

Miniature model of the ancient capital Heian-kyō

Heian-kyō was built in what is now the central part of Kyoto city, covering an area spanning the Kadono ([葛野郡](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%91%9B%E9%87%8E%E9%83%A1 "ja:葛野郡"), *Kadono-gun*) and Otagi Districts ([愛宕郡](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%84%9B%E5%AE%95%E9%83%A1 "ja:愛宕郡"), *Otagi-gun*) of [Yamashiro Province](/wiki/Yamashiro_Province "Yamashiro Province"). The city boundaries formed a rectangle measuring 4.5 km from east to west and 5.2 km from north to south. The city layout followed [Heijō-kyō](/wiki/Heij%C5%8D-ky%C5%8D "Heijō-kyō") (Japan's capital during the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period")) with the Imperial palace, [Daidairi](/wiki/Heian_Palace "Heian Palace"), placed in the center of the northern city limits and the [Suzaku Avenue](/wiki/Suzaku_Avenue "Suzaku Avenue") (Suzaku-ōji), the main thoroughfare extending from the palace down through the center of the city, dividing it into the Right ([Ukyō](/wiki/Uky%C5%8D-ku,_Kyoto "Ukyō-ku, Kyoto")) and Left Capitals ([Sakyō](/wiki/Saky%C5%8D-ku,_Kyoto "Sakyō-ku, Kyoto")) (the eastern side being the Left and the western side being the Right from the emperor's viewpoint.) The design followed [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") and Tang dynasty Chang'an, with the exception that Heian-kyō had no city walls. It is thought that the site for the city was selected according to the principles of Shijinsōō ([四神相応](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%9B%9B%E7%A5%9E%E7%9B%B8%E5%BF%9C "ja:四神相応"); "Four Gods Suitability") based on Chinese [Feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") and relating to the [Four Symbols](/wiki/Four_Symbols "Four Symbols") of Chinese astrology.

The boundaries of Heian-kyō were smaller than those of modern Kyoto, with Ichijō-ōji (一条大路) at the northern limit corresponding to present-day Ichijō-dōri ([一条通](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%80%E6%9D%A1%E9%80%9A "ja:一条通")), between [Imadegawa-dōri](/wiki/Imadegawa_Street "Imadegawa Street") (今出川通) and Marutamachi-dōri ([丸太町通](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%B8%E5%A4%AA%E7%94%BA%E9%80%9A "ja:丸太町通")), Kyūjō-ōji in the south corresponding to Kujō-dōri ([九条通](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B9%9D%E6%9D%A1%E9%80%9A "ja:九条通")), slightly to the south of the present-day [JR](/wiki/JR_West "JR West") [Kyōto Station](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dto_Station "Kyōto Station"), and Higashi-kyōgoku-ōji in the east corresponding to present-day [Teramachi Street](/wiki/Teramachi_Street "Teramachi Street") (Teramachi-dōri). The location of Nishi-kyōgoku-ōji at the western limit is estimated as a line running north to south from [Hanazono Station](/wiki/Hanazono_Station_(Kyoto) "Hanazono Station (Kyoto)") on the JR [San'in Main Line](/wiki/San%27in_Main_Line "San'in Main Line") to [Nishi-Kyōgoku Station](/wiki/Nishi-Ky%C5%8Dgoku_Station "Nishi-Kyōgoku Station") on the [Hankyu Kyoto Line](/wiki/Hankyu_Kyoto_Line "Hankyu Kyoto Line").

The layout of Heian-kyō was plotted in accordance with the principles of [geomancy](/wiki/Geomancy "Geomancy") as a square city. Jō (丈; about 3.03m) was the basic unit of measurement. 40 square jō made a chō (町; 121.2 meters on each side). The city was further divided by major streets called ōji (大路) and minor streets called koji (小路). Four lines of chō running east to west (excepting the first 2 rows in the north) were together called a jō (条) and four lines of chō running from north to south were called a bō (坊). The chō which shared the same jō and bō were each given a number from 1 to 16. In this way addresses could be identified with the following arrangement: "Right Capital, jō Five, bō Two, chō Fourteen" (右京五条二坊十四町).

The width of even the minor streets was 4 jō (丈; about 12m), and for the major streets, over 8 jō (丈; about 24m). Almost all of the streets in present-day Kyoto have become considerably narrower. [Suzaku-ōji](/wiki/Suzaku_Avenue "Suzaku Avenue"), for example, was 28 jō (丈; about 84m) wide. In addition, a river ran alongside [Horikawa-koji](/wiki/Horikawa,_Kyoto "Horikawa, Kyoto") (堀川小路) and Nishi Horikawa-koji (西堀川小路).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heian-ky%C5%8D&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/1696_Genroku_9_%28early_Edo%29_Japanese_Map_of_Kyoto%2C_Japan_-_Geographicus_-_Kyoto-genroku9-1696.jpg/250px-1696_Genroku_9_%28early_Edo%29_Japanese_Map_of_Kyoto%2C_Japan_-_Geographicus_-_Kyoto-genroku9-1696.jpg)](/wiki/File:1696_Genroku_9_(early_Edo)_Japanese_Map_of_Kyoto,_Japan_-_Geographicus_-_Kyoto-genroku9-1696.jpg)

Map of Heian-kyō, 1696

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Toshi-ya_00.jpg/250px-Toshi-ya_00.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toshi-ya_00.jpg)

*Perspective Pictures of Places in Japan: [Sanjūsangen-dō](/wiki/Sanj%C5%ABsangen-d%C5%8D "Sanjūsangen-dō") in Kyoto*  
[Toyoharu](/wiki/Utagawa_Toyoharu "Utagawa Toyoharu"), c. 1772–1781

In 784 AD, Emperor Kammu constructed [Nagaoka-kyō](/wiki/Nagaoka-ky%C5%8D "Nagaoka-kyō"), moving the capital from Heijō-kyō. It is thought that he wished to build a new, *[Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Emperor_Tenji "Emperor Tenji") faction* capital far from [Yamato Province](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province"), which was the power base for the temples and aristocrats who supported the *[Emperor Tenmu](/wiki/Emperor_Tenmu "Emperor Tenmu") faction*. However, only 9 years later in January 793 AD, Emperor Kammu assembled his retainers and announced another relocation of the capital to address outstanding issues experienced in Nagaoka-kyō. The location for the new capital was to be in Kadono, located between two rivers in the north of Yamashiro, ten kilometers to the northeast of Nagaoka-kyō. It is said that Emperor Kammu had previously looked out on Kadono from the Shōgun Tsuka in [Higashiyama Ward](/wiki/Higashiyama-ku,_Kyoto "Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto") of Kyoto City, deciding then that it was a suitable location for the capital. Emperor Kammu's words are recorded in the *[Nihon Kiryaku](/wiki/Nihon_Kiryaku "Nihon Kiryaku")* as follows: "Kadono has beautiful mountains and rivers as well as good transport links by sea and land making it convenient for people to assemble there from all four corners of the country."

It is thought that the construction of Heian-kyō began from the palace, with the construction of the remainder of the city following afterwards. As a display of the emperor's authority, the Daigokuden (main building of the palace) was constructed at the far north of the central thoroughfare, Suzaku-oji, making the building visible from anywhere in the city.

Ports such as Yodonotsu (淀津; now [Yodo](/wiki/Yodo-juku "Yodo-juku")) and Ōitsu (大井津) were set up along the river next to the city. These ports acted as a transit base for collecting goods from all over the country and forwarding them on to the city. The goods which arrived in Heian-kyō reached the people by way of one of the two large markets: the East Market and the West Market. This arrangement provided a stable supply of food and goods which encouraged population growth. Measures were also taken to guard against the flooding which had plagued the residents of Nagaoka-kyō. Although there was no natural river in the center of Heian-kyō, two artificial canals (the present day Horikawa and Nishi Horikawa) were dug, boasting adjustable water levels, simultaneously securing a supply of water and guarding against flooding.

Similar to the previous capital of Nagaoka-kyō, the construction of [Buddhist temples](/wiki/Buddhist_temples "Buddhist temples") in Heian-kyō was formally forbidden, the only exceptions being the East ([Tō-ji](/wiki/T%C5%8D-ji "Tō-ji")) and West ([Sai-ji](/wiki/Sai-ji "Sai-ji")) temples, with the thought that their power might protect the city from natural disaster and disease, and priests such as [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai") were welcomed, being able men, well versed in Buddhist scripture, and with no interest in political power.

On 22 October 794 AD, Emperor Kammu arrived at the new city and on 8 November proclaimed, "I hereby name this city Heian-kyō." On 8 November, he changed the second [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") character of Yamashiro from 背 ("back") to 城 ("castle") because the capital looked like a naturally formed "mountain castle" surrounded by the Eastern (Higashiyama), Northern (Kitayama), and Western (Nishiyama) mountains.

A movement in favor of returning the capital to Heijō-kyō arose in 810 AD, during a standoff over the emperor's succession. However, [Emperor Saga](/wiki/Emperor_Saga "Emperor Saga") thought that keeping the capital in Heian-kyō would be best for the stability of the country and resisted this movement, declaring Heian-kyō "The Eternal Capital" (万代宮, *"Yorozuyo no Miya"*).

The land of the Right Capital overlapped the wetlands formed by the [Katsura River](/wiki/Katsura_River "Katsura River") and, even by the 9th century, little progress had been made in developing the area. By the 10th century, when the [Ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō") system was almost at an end, the district had become so dilapidated that it began to be used as farmland, something which had previously been forbidden within the city limits. With the exception of an area in the north of the Right Capital near the palace, the residential areas which housed the aristocracy were all situated in the Left Capital, with the highest echelon of aristocrats such as the [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan") gathering in the northernmost part of the district.

The poor of Heian-kyō began to set up home by the [Kamo River](/wiki/Kamo_River "Kamo River"), beyond the eastern limits of the city, and on the eastern banks of the river, temples and country homes sprang up. So started a tendency for the city to extend out to the east. In 980 AD, at the southern tip of Suzaku-oji, the [Rajōmon](/wiki/Raj%C5%8Dmon "Rajōmon") (the grandest of the two city gates) collapsed, never to be rebuilt. In this way, the original borders of Heian-kyō extended out to the east, forming the streets of first medieval and then modern-day Kyoto.

With the advent of the [Kanto](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region#History "Kantō region")-centered [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate") and [Edo shogunates](/wiki/Edo_shogunate "Edo shogunate"), Heian-kyō began to lose its significance as a seat of power. The greatest decline was during the [Muromachi](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period") and [Sengoku periods](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), when almost half the city was burnt to the ground during the [Ōnin war](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_war "Ōnin war"). After this, Heian-kyō separated into upper ([Kamigyō](/wiki/Kamigy%C5%8D-ku "Kamigyō-ku")) and lower ([Shimogyō](/wiki/Shimogy%C5%8D-ku,_Kyoto "Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto")) cities, each becoming places of little note. However, the two were to be reunited into one city during the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period") following the ascension of [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga").

During the [Meiji Revolution](/wiki/Meiji_Revolution "Meiji Revolution"), Edo was re-christened Tokyo (becoming the new capital of Japan). Although Heian-kyō lost its status as capital city, it became a backup capital while the emperor was away in Tokyo. Since that time, the emperor has not returned to Kyoto. However, at the direction of Emperor Meiji, the imperial residences have been preserved and the *takamikura* ([高御座](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%AB%98%E5%BE%A1%E5%BA%A7 "ja:高御座"))—a special throne whose location traditionally marked the seat of the emperor—remains at the palace in Kyoto.

## Schematic diagram of Heian-kyō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heian-ky%C5%8D&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Schematic diagram of Heian-kyō")]

The green areas in the diagram are markets, temples, and a garden. There were two large markets, West Market (西市) and East Market (東市), facing the seventh street, [Shichijō](/w/index.php?title=Shichij%C5%8D_Street&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shichijō Street (page does not exist)")-ōji (七条大路). Tō-ji (東寺; "East Temple") and Sai-ji (西寺; "West Temple") were Buddhist temples built on the southern edge of the capital. An imperial garden called Shinsenen (神泉苑) was adjacent to the Daidairi.

Note that there were more paths cut short by residences straddling multiple blocks.

[![Schematic diagram of Heian-kyō](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/18/Heiankyo_map.png)](/wiki/File:Heiankyo_map.png "Schematic diagram of Heian-kyō")

In English (major streets and palace only):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/63/Heiankyo_palace_location.png)](/wiki/File:Heiankyo_palace_location.png)
